attributes.stem: A 13-year-old boy presents to his General Practice clinic with his mother with a three-week history of a limp and increasing left knee pain. He first noticed the pain after playing soccer at school three weeks ago. There is no recent history of trauma or previous episodes of joint pain. He describes the pain as being located in his left knee, radiating to his left thigh and groin. The pain has progressively worsened and he now finds it difficult to weight bear on his left lower limb. He has no significant medical history and is up to date with immunisations. On examination, he appears overweight with a BMI of 39 kg/m2. He is uncomfortable and walks with an antalgic gait. His left lower limb is shortened and externally rotated during flexion of the hip.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
options: Reassure and advise the pain should self-resolve within a few days
Prescribe analgesia and advise to return if pain persists beyond one week
Prescribe analgesia and antibiotics, with GP follow-up in 48 hours
Prescribe analgesia and order an outpatient left knee X-ray
attributes.explanation:
This is a presentation of slipped upper femoral epiphysis (SUFE). SUFE occurs when there is a fracture of the growth plate below the head of the femur leading to dislocation of the femoral head. It usually occurs in growing adolescents, with obesity being one of the biggest risk factors for SUFE. When SUFE is suspected, the child must be made non-weight bearing and an urgent orthopaedic assessment arranged. Although this boy is able to weight bear with difficulty, the inability to weight bear would warrant prompt referral to the Emergency Department for an urgent assessment. In these circumstances, the patient would likely be operated on as an emergency.

Although the clinical scenario is highly suspicious for SUFE, there are a range of differentials for knee pain in the paediatric population. Knee pain associated with exercise can also be a result of iliotibial band (ITB) syndrome, which would be suspected in an individual who runs frequently or has recently commenced regular running. However, it is unlikely for children with ITB syndrome to present with difficulty weight-bearing. Simple analgesia and activity modification may be appropriate for milder cases of overuse injuries like ITB syndrome, but the inability to weight bear makes this less likely.

Osteochondritis dissecans is another differential for knee pain in adolescents. Osteochondritis presents with knee pain associated with an effusion. It occurs due to insufficient blood flow affecting the subchondral bone and articular cartilage of the knee. An X-ray would be helpful in diagnosing this condition, but the history and examination findings make this less likely than SUFE.

A painful joint in children can also be due to reactive arthritis. The clinical picture is often painful joints in the days or weeks following a viral illness. It is a diagnosis of exclusion and can be managed with simple analgesia and rest. However, the prolonged duration of symptoms and examination findings are atypical for reactive arthritis.

It is also critical to rule out septic arthritis in children with acute knee pain, as a missed diagnosis of septic arthritis can be fatal. If septic arthritis was strongly suspected, empiric antibiotic therapy would be warranted. However, the subacute onset and lack of systemic features like fever make septic arthritis less likely than SUFE in this case.
learningPoints: When there is suspected SUFE, the child must be made non-weight bearing and urgent orthopaedic assessment should be made.
references: The Royal Children's Hospital. Slipped upper femoral epiphysis SUFE - Emergency Department [Internet]. Melbourne: The Royal Children's Hospital; [updated 2020 Dec; cited 2022 Sep 06]. Available from: https://www.rch.org.au/clinicalguide/guideline_index/fractures/Slipped_upper_femoral_epiphysis_SUFE_-_Emergency_Department/

Weigall P, Vladusic S, Torode I. Slipped upper femoral epiphysis: Delays to diagnosis. AFP [Internet]. 2010 Mar [cited 2022 Sep 06]; 39(3):151-3. Available from: https://www.racgp.org.au/getattachment/c7df3664-1065-4832-8050-778c8725677f/Slipped-upper-femoral-epiphysis-in-children-delays.aspx

The Royal Children's Hospital. The Limping or Non-Weight Bearing child. Melbourne: The Royal Children's Hospital 2020. Cited January 2023 . Available from: https://www.rch.org.au/clinicalguide/guideline_index/Child_with_limp/
Huguenin L. Paediatric sports injuries. Australian family physician. 2016 Jul;45(7):466-9.
